architecture.summary: The paper interprets GPT-2's residual stream as a vector symbolic architecture (VSA) where nearly orthogonal concept vectors are bundled (summed) and bound (matrix-multiplied) across layers. Word embeddings serve as initial concept vectors; attention layers perform unbinding-bundling-rebinding operations to focus on specific token packages and aggregate their similarities; MLP blocks implement boolean functions (OR, AND, NOT) on bundled concept vectors by checking weighted sums against thresholds, with outputs written back to the stream as new nearly orthogonal concepts. Layer normalization and biases support near-zero mean maintenance and auxiliary 'cleaning', while the unembedding projects residual directions back to token probabilities via dot products with word embeddings.

biblio.title: GPT-2 Through the Lens of Vector Symbolic Architectures

claims.statement: Word embeddings of GPT-2 are nearly orthogonal to each other and can be interpreted as concept vectors.

Attention layers can be interpreted as a sequence of unbinding operations (Q-transformation), bundling of selected vectors based on relevance, and rebinding (output projection), effectively focusing on and repackaging specific concept packages.
More than 80% of neurons in the first MLP layer have weights that can be explained by simple bundled vectors (sums of word embeddings) with a cosine similarity of 0.7 or higher.
At least 95% of neurons in the first MLP layer can be explained by bundled vectors with a cosine similarity of at least 0.5, including attributions to attention heads.

30–50% of neurons in middle layers can be explained by bundled vectors composed of output projection vectors from attention and MLP blocks with a cosine similarity of at least 0.7, with most bundlings composed of up to 40 vectors.
Attention and MLP output projection matrices are composed of nearly orthogonal vectors, as evidenced by strong diagonals with minimal off-diagonal artifacts in M^T M products.
The mean of word embeddings and intermediate hidden embeddings across multiple prompts is near zero, suggesting low angular distortion of concept directions across layers despite layer normalization.
Experiments are limited to GPT-2 small; further experiments are needed to confirm that these bundling and binding behaviors also exist in related and more recent models.
